lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Yet better thus, and known to be contemn’d,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Than still contemn’d and flatter’d. To be worst,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The lowest and most dejected thing of fortune,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Stands still in esperance, lives not in fear:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The lamentable change is from the best;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The worst returns to laughter. Welcome, then,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Thou unsubstantial air that I embrace!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The wretch that thou hast blown unto the worst

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Owes nothing to thy blasts. But who comes here?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: My father, poorly led? World, world, O world!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: But that thy strange mutations make us hate thee,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Lie would not yield to age.

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: O, my good lord, I have been your tenant, and your father’s

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: tenant, these fourscore years.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Away, get thee away; good friend, be gone:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thy comforts can do me no good at all;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thee they may hurt.

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Alack, sir, you cannot see your way.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I have no way, and therefore want no eyes;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I stumbled when I saw: full oft ’tis seen,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Our means secure us, and our mere defects

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Prove our commodities. O dear son Edgar,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The food of thy abused father’s wrath!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Might I but live to see thee in my touch,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ld say I had eyes again!

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: How now! Who’s there?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: O gods! Who is’t can say “I am at the worst”?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: I am worse than e’er I was.

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: ’Tis poor mad Tom.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: And worse I may be yet: the worst is not

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: So long as we can say “This is the worst.”

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Fellow, where goest?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Is it a beggar-man?

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Madman and beggar too.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He has some reason, else he could not beg.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ the last night’s storm I such a fellow saw;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which made me think a man a worm: my son

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Came then into my mind; and yet my mind

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Was then scarce friends with him: I have heard more since.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As flies to wanton boys, are we to the gods.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: They kill us for their sport.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: How should this be?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Bad is the trade that must play fool to sorrow,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Angering itself and others.—Bless thee, master!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Is that the naked fellow?

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Ay, my lord.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Then, prithee, get thee gone: if, for my sake,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thou wilt o’ertake us, hence a mile or twain,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ the way toward Dover, do it for ancient love;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And bring some covering for this naked soul,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Who I’ll entreat to lead me.

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Alack, sir, he is mad.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’Tis the times’ plague, when madmen lead the blind.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Do as I bid thee, or rather do thy pleasure;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Above the rest, be gone.

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: I’ll bring him the best ’parel that I have,

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Come on’t what will.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Sirrah, naked fellow—

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Poor Tom’s a-cold. I cannot daub it further.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Come hither, fellow.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: And yet I must.—Bless thy sweet eyes, they bleed.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Know’st thou the way to Dover?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Both stile and gate, horse-way and foot-path. Poor Tom hath been scared

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: out of his good wits: bless thee, good man’s son, from the

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: foul fiend! five fiends have been in poor Tom at once; of

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: lust, as Obidicut; Hobbididence, prince of dumbness; Mahu, of stealing; Modo, of

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: murder; Flibbertigibbet, of mopping and mowing, who since possesses chambermaids and waiting-women.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: So, bless thee, master!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Here, take this purse, thou whom the heavens’ plagues

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Have humbled to all strokes: that I am wretched

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Makes thee the happier: heavens, deal so still!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Let the superfluous and lust-dieted man,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That slaves your ordinance, that will not see

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Because he doth not feel, feel your power quickly;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So distribution should undo excess,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And each man have enough. Dost thou know Dover?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Ay, master.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: There is a cliff, whose high and bending head

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Looks fearfully in the confined deep:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Bring me but to the very brim of it,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And I’ll repair the misery thou dost bear

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With something rich about me: from that place

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I shall no leading need.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Give me thy arm:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Poor Tom shall lead thee.

stage_directions.text: Enter Gloucester, led by an Old Man.

glosses.definition: despised
object abased by fortune
a subject for laughter — darkly ironic
our comforts make us careless
we are not at the worst / as long as we can say / 'This is the worst'
wanton in the old sense — undisciplined, cruel-playful
long-standing affection
Gloucester's command — concerned for Tom's safety
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — recurs here from 3.4
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — a devil of lust
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — recurs from 3.4
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — recurs from 3.4
lust-fed — the luxurious who live on desire
enslaves God's ordained order
the over-fed rich — Gloucester's echo of Lear's 3.4 speech
wealth-sharing should erase excess — Gloucester's second echo of Lear
to be openly despised
caught between contempt and flattery

hope

even worse now than at first
Gloucester's gift to Tom
piteous creature
the overrich — Gloucester's echo of Lear's 3.4 speech

annotations.title: 'As flies to wanton boys' — the play's theological centerpiece
annotations.body: Gloucester's 'As flies to wanton boys are we to the gods; / They kill us for their sport' is the most direct theological statement in Shakespeare. The image is borrowed from Plutarch and Montaigne, but Shakespeare's concentration is new: the gods are not merely indifferent, they are cruel-playful, like boys tearing the wings off insects. This is theological skepticism in its sharpest Jacobean form. Gloucester has just been blinded; he has lost his son through his own folly; he is being led across an open country by a beggar he does not recognise as Edgar. The image is earned. Critics have read Lear as Shakespeare's most religious play and as his most secular. Gloucester's line is a test case: a character in extremity voicing a cosmology in which the divine order is hostile. The play does not refute him. Gloucester's restoration in 5.3 (his heart 'burst smilingly') happens offstage, and even that is ambiguous. Virgil's verdict: Shakespeare does not finally arbitrate, but the weight of the line is that of a real question, not a rhetorical flourish.
sources: William R. Elton, King Lear and the Gods (1966)

annotations.title: The demons return — Flibbertigibbet, Obidicut, Hobbididance, Mahu, Modo
annotations.body: Edgar's recital of demon-names here extends the Harsnett catalogue from 3.4. Five devils named in six lines: Flibbertigibbet (from 3.4), Obidicut (of lust), Hobbididance (dumbness), Mahu (stealing), Modo (murder), plus 'of mopping and mowing' (grimacing). This is the full taxonomy from Harsnett's Declaration, which Shakespeare is using to give Poor Tom a consistent demonological vocabulary. The demons are assigned specific sins, making Tom's ravings a mock-catechism of vice. Edgar is playing along with Gloucester's belief that a demon led him to the cliff; by naming demons from an exposed-fraud source, Shakespeare doubles the irony — the demons are theatrical props all the way down.
sources: Samuel Harsnett, A Declaration of Egregious Popish Impostures (1603)
Stephen Greenblatt, 'Shakespeare and the Exorcists' in Shakespearean Negotiations (1988)

annotations.title: Gloucester's redistributive ethics — 'Distribution should undo excess'
annotations.body: Blind Gloucester, giving Tom his purse: 'Heavens, deal so still! / Let the superfluous and lust-dieted man, / That slaves your ordinance, that will not see / Because he does not feel, feel your power quickly; / So distribution should undo excess, / And each man have enough.' Gloucester's speech rhymes almost verbatim with Lear's 'Poor naked wretches' in 3.4. Both blind men (Lear in his madness, Gloucester in his eyes) reach the same redistributive conclusion: luxury should be redistributed to need, not hoarded. This is the play's most compressed political theology. Shakespeare has made the argument available in both plots: it is the moral conclusion drawn by the play's most radically suffering figures, in parallel, independently.

annotations.title: 'The worst is not / So long as we can say, This is the worst'
annotations.body: Edgar's aside — 'O gods! Who is't can say I am at the worst? / I am worse than e'er I was.... / The worst is not / So long as we can say, this is the worst' — is the play's most painfully recursive thought. Language can always name a new bottom, which means the bottom has not been reached. This is the inverse of consolation: the very capacity to articulate one's condition proves it is not yet final. Edgar has just seen his blinded father led by an old man; he has nowhere lower left to go, except that by saying so he demonstrates he does. The paradox is a gloss on the play's entire progression — each time we think the worst has come, another worse arrives. Cordelia's death in 5.3 is what finally silences the capacity to articulate; there is no this is the worst after her body appears.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

trials.prompt: What does Gloucester's 'As flies to wanton boys' claim?
options: That the gods are not merely indifferent but cruel-playful, treating humans as boys treat insects — the play's sharpest statement of theological skepticism

trials.prompt: How does Gloucester's 'distribution should undo excess' speech relate to Lear's 'poor naked wretches' speech?
options: They are parallel — both blind men reaching the same redistributive conclusion independently; Shakespeare doubles the moral argument across both plots

trials.prompt: What paradox does Edgar articulate in his 'the worst is not so long as we can say this is the worst' aside?
options: That the worst is always behind us
Language's capacity to name a condition proves that condition is not final — the bottom has not been reached while we can still say so; the inverse of consolation
